capabilityFacts.value: OpusClip turns long-form source videos into short clips and can publish them directly to social platforms.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The main product page and help center both describe a long-video to shorts workflow with one-click publishing.

capabilityFacts.value: ClipAnything uses visual, audio, and sentiment cues to find clips even when there is little dialogue.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The ClipAnything article explains that the model analyzes frames through visual, audio, and sentiment cues and works on videos with little or no dialogue.

capabilityFacts.value: Users can prompt for specific moments, actions, characters, emotions, or viral moments during clipping.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The usage guide tells users to select ClipAnything and enter natural-language prompts to find specific scenes and events.

capabilityFacts.value: OpusClip auto-generates captions and reframes clips across 9:16, 1:1, and 16:9 outputs.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: Both the pricing article and ClipAnything docs call out captions and the three main aspect ratios.

capabilityFacts.value: The platform exposes AI B-roll, AI audio enhance, and AI voice-over in the core product surface.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: These capabilities are listed directly on the official home page feature rail.

capabilityFacts.value: The free plan includes 60 processing minutes per month, captions, YouTube and local imports, and one brand template.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The plans page itemizes the Free Forever plan entitlements.

capabilityFacts.value: Watermark-free exports are a paid capability; downgrading to free reintroduces watermarked videos.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The downgrade section explicitly warns that videos will be watermarked on the free plan.

capabilityFacts.value: Self-serve team workspace tops out at 4 seats; larger teams are pushed to contact sales.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The Team Management article says Pro packs allow up to 4 seats and asks users with larger needs to contact OpusClip.

capabilityFacts.value: The current team workspace does not include an internal chat or note system.
capabilityFacts.evidenceText: The FAQ in Team Management explicitly says this is not supported in the current version.

modelSupportItems.scope: Multimodal clip selection in web app
modelSupportItems.evidenceText: OpusClip presents ClipAnything as its multimodal AI clipping model and instructs users to select it under Clip model.
The ClipAnything article describes ReframeAnything as the model that keeps key objects centered across resized outputs.
The home page describes OpusClip as using AI editing models that understand every pixel of a video.
The reviewed official pages expose OpusClip's own clipping and reframe models, but do not show a user-facing third-party model catalog or provider picker.

uniqueValueItems.summary: OpusClip's main differentiator is not generic generation but automated repurposing of existing long-form footage into short-form output that is already formatted for social channels.
uniqueValueItems.evidenceText: Official pages are centered on long video ingestion, clip discovery, captions, reframe, publishing, and feed distribution rather than creating scenes from scratch.

uniqueValueItems.summary: ClipAnything stands out because it is positioned to work on genres where dialogue alone is a weak signal, such as sports, gaming, and vlogs.
uniqueValueItems.evidenceText: The ClipAnything article repeatedly stresses visual, audio, and sentiment cues plus support for low-dialogue video.

differentiationItems.positioning: repurposing tool with workflow automation and publishing layers
differentiationItems.evidenceText: The product pages treat clipping, reframing, captioning, and publishing as the core motion, with generation features acting as assistive layers.

differentiationItems.positioning: Best where the input is existing video inventory and the goal is faster distribution, not where teams need full scene generation or deep collaborative review.
differentiationItems.evidenceText: Official pages lead with long-video inputs and social outputs, while team review features are comparatively narrow.

collaborationItems.value: Team workspaces share credits, videos, and brand templates across the team.
collaborationItems.evidenceText: The Team Management article explicitly says teams can share credits, videos, and brand templates.

collaborationItems.value: Owners can manage members and subscriptions; members can create using shared credits but cannot manage billing or the team.
collaborationItems.evidenceText: The permissions FAQ distinguishes owner capabilities from member capabilities.

collaborationItems.value: Granular role assignment is not fully available yet and is marked as a future capability.
collaborationItems.evidenceText: The permissions section says the ability to assign diverse roles and permissions is a future capability.

developerItems.value: The API supports create-project, query-clips, brand template, share-project, and webhook flows.
developerItems.evidenceText: The API overview enumerates the core endpoints and project lifecycle.

developerItems.value: The API is rate-limited at 30 requests per minute per key, supports up to 10-hour videos, 30 GB files, and 50 concurrent projects.
developerItems.evidenceText: The API overview publishes rate, duration, size, storage, and concurrency limits.

developerItems.value: API callers can list brand templates and create projects with a chosen template id.
developerItems.evidenceText: The brand-template API docs show listing templates and passing a brandTemplateId in project creation.

commercialItems.value: Starter adds social posting to YouTube, TikTok, and Instagram, while Pro adds more channels and scheduling.
commercialItems.evidenceText: The plans page itemizes posting and scheduling capabilities by plan.

commercialItems.value: Pro packs include 100 GB cloud storage, and API projects expire after 30 days unless autosave is enabled.
commercialItems.evidenceText: The plans page lists Pro cloud storage and the API overview explains default 30-day project expiration.

commercialItems.value: OpusClip markets SOC 2 Type II compliance on its API page.
commercialItems.evidenceText: The API landing page explicitly says enterprise-grade security with SOC 2 Type II compliance.

limitationItems.value: Self-serve usage is primarily limited by monthly or yearly processing minutes.
limitationItems.evidenceText: The plans page anchors every tier to processing minutes rather than unlimited usage.

limitationItems.value: Extra seats cannot be purchased independently; users must buy another pack that bundles seats, credits, templates, and storage.
limitationItems.evidenceText: The plans FAQ explicitly says extra seats cannot be purchased on their own.

limitationItems.value: Team workspaces burn the owner's credits, not the member's own subscription credits.
limitationItems.evidenceText: The team FAQ explicitly says workspace activity consumes the owner's credits.

useCaseItems.fit: Strong fit when the main job is clipping long podcast or interview inventory into short-form output.
useCaseItems.evidenceText: The default workflow assumes a long video source and short social outputs with captions and reframing.

useCaseItems.useCase: sports, gaming, or vlog highlight mining
useCaseItems.fit: ClipAnything is particularly suited to footage where non-dialogue moments matter.
useCaseItems.evidenceText: Official examples stress sports, travel, gaming, and other visual moment detection scenarios.

useCaseItems.fit: The API is useful when teams need clip generation, captioning, reframing, and publishing inside an automated distribution stack.
useCaseItems.evidenceText: The API landing page and reference emphasize autonomous workflows, multilingual distribution, webhooks, and scalable feed publishing.

userFitItems.fit: Strong fit for creators whose bottleneck is turning one long recording into many short posts quickly.
userFitItems.rationale: Free and Starter plans already center on import, captions, and social export rather than full editing suites.
userFitItems.evidenceText: The official plan ladder starts with processing minutes, imports, captions, and simple brand templates.

userFitItems.fit: Useful for small teams that share a clip pipeline, though collaboration is operational rather than review-centric.
userFitItems.rationale: The team workspace shares credits and assets, but lacks chat and deep review tooling.
userFitItems.evidenceText: Team management focuses on membership, credits, and shared assets; several collaboration niceties are explicitly unsupported.

userFitItems.fit: Fit increases when teams need high-volume API clipping, sales-assisted seats, or more than the self-serve workspace limits.
userFitItems.rationale: Business and API access route users to contact sales, which signals a higher-touch operational tier.
userFitItems.evidenceText: The plans page moves larger seat counts, large volume usage, custom solutions, and API into the Business contact flow.

rawEvidenceBlocks.evidenceText: Official positioning centers on converting one long video into many clips and publishing them to social channels.
ClipAnything is documented as multimodal and explicitly useful for low-dialogue content.
Minutes, watermark removal, source integrations, social posting, and team access are all plan-gated.
The API has explicit per-key rate limits, project concurrency caps, file size limits, and default project expiry.
Collaboration exists through shared assets and owner-managed workspaces, but not through mature review or communication features.

unresolvedQuestions.question: Is there a public admin or audit log feature for enterprise workspace governance beyond owner-member basics?
unresolvedQuestions.reason: The reviewed pages show basic ownership and seat rules, but not a full enterprise governance surface.

unresolvedQuestions.question: Does OpusClip publish a formal commercial licensing or content ownership page for generated captions, AI B-roll, and voice-over outputs?
unresolvedQuestions.reason: The reviewed product and help pages focus on workflow and plans, not rights language.
